channel.title: Literature on Pieter Belmans
channel.description: Recent content in Literature on Pieter Belmans

item.title: More 'Infinite Jest' web goodies
item.description:
My love for David Foster Wallace's Infinite Jest has been expressed before, where I linked to the wonderful tumblr Poor Yorick Entertainment. Now there is something new: an involved art project by William Beutler based on Infinite Jest. There is:

  • Infinite Atlas which maps all locations used in the book on Google Maps;
  • Infinite Boston which contains a really thorough discussion of locations in Infinite Jest, including photographs!
  • Infinite Shop where you can buy Infinite Jest related stuff if you feel like supporting the project.

With all this interesting stuff I feel a reread coming up.

item.title: A visual exploration of the filmography of James O. Incandenza and the world of David Foster Wallace's "Infinite Jest."
item.description:
Because that's what it is.

When reading Infinite Jest I always wondered whether someone would ever make all the fake cultural references real. This thought occurred to me several times, when imaginary fiction or movies were mentioned in a book. But Wallace's universe in Infinite Jest is so immensely littered with non-existing movie plots it really screams for elaboration. Which is now being done, hurray!

In case you haven't read Infinite Jest and you are looking for a tip for your summer reading: please consider it. It is amazing.

item.title: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland, or 3 math novels
item.description: Let's start at the beginning. I was reading Don DeLillo's Ratner's Star. As a great fan of DeLillo I was excited to read this one as it is about a mathematician in a sci-fi setting. That sounds like 2 great things combined. Unfortunately, this is either a bad novel or I don't understand it. But surely it is a false dichotomy! Which is my favourite logical fallacy by the way.
